title: Memory cards don't wrap on narrow viewports — horizontal overflow
analysis: The memory partial (memory.html line 9) uses `flex gap-4` without `flex-wrap`, causing four health cards to stay side-by-side and crush on narrow viewports. Each card uses `flex-1` but has no minimum width, so they shrink to unreadable sizes. The fix is two Tailwind class additions: (1) `flex-wrap` on the container div, and (2) `min-w-[200px]` on each of the four card divs. The existing test suite (test_memory_partial.py) uses starlette TestClient with patched MCP calls and checks rendered HTML string content — the same pattern will be used for the new layout assertions. There are exactly four card divs with class `bg-gray-800 rounded-lg p-6 shadow flex-1` (Graphiti, Mem0, Taskmaster, Write Queue).

steps.description: Write failing tests in test_memory_partial.py: (1) assert the card container div includes 'flex-wrap' in its class list, (2) assert all four card divs include 'min-w-[200px]' class. Add a new TestMemoryCardLayout class with two test methods following the existing patch pattern.

Update memory.html line 9: change `<div class="flex gap-4">` to `<div class="flex flex-wrap gap-4">`. Update each of the four card divs (lines 11, 21, 31, 44) to add `min-w-[200px]` — changing `bg-gray-800 rounded-lg p-6 shadow flex-1` to `bg-gray-800 rounded-lg p-6 shadow flex-1 min-w-[200px]`.

design_decisions.decision: Use min-w-[200px] rather than a responsive grid (e.g., grid-cols-2)
design_decisions.rationale: The task description specifies min-w-[200px] with flex-wrap for natural wrapping. This is simpler than a grid approach and lets cards wrap to 2x2 or 1-column naturally based on available space, matching the PRD intent of graceful degradation without full mobile support.

flex-1 allows cards to grow and fill available space on wide viewports, while min-w-[200px] prevents them from shrinking below readable width. Together they give the desired behavior: equal-width cards that wrap when constrained.

design_decisions.decision: Test via HTML string assertions rather than visual/browser tests
design_decisions.rationale: The existing test suite checks rendered HTML classes via string matching (e.g., 'bg-green-500' in html). CSS class presence is sufficient to verify the fix since the actual layout behavior is defined by Tailwind's well-tested utility classes. Browser-based layout testing would be disproportionate for this change.

reuse.how: Reuse the same _ONLINE_STATUS, _ONLINE_QUEUE fixtures and the dual-patch context manager pattern to render the memory partial and inspect the HTML output.
Use the existing client() fixture that creates a Starlette TestClient for the dashboard app.
